query: Growth pattern of the maxilla during adolescence

retrieved_docs.title: Craniofacial Distraction Osteogenesis -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Normal Craniofacial Development
retrieved_docs.content: The nasomaxillary complex normally grows anterior and inferiorly from the basicranium as a result of soft-tissue growth. Midface maturity is reached at about 12 years. As the nasomaxillary complex grows, the mandible concomitantly protrudes in the downward and forward directions. This results from the increasing size of the muscles of mastication and the developing oropharynx. The mandible does not reach adult size in males until well after puberty, into their early twenties. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Craniofacial Distraction Osteogenesis -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Normal Craniofacial Development
retrieved_docs.content: As an infant grows into childhood and adulthood, the craniofacial proportions change. To illustrate how normal growth occurs, the craniofacial region is broken up into three general regions: the neurocranium (further divided into the calvaria and basicranium), the nasomaxillary complex, and the mandible. In infants, the neurocranium is much larger relative to the nasomaxillary complex and the mandible. As demand for the masticatory and respiratory systems grows, the nose, mouth, and mandible grow considerably to more closely resemble the proportions of the adult face.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Macrodontia often presents an aesthetic concern in the anterior maxilla, while macrodonts in the posterior region may contribute to crowding and impede normal tooth eruption. Radiographic monitoring is essential to assess potential eruption abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Fibrous Dysplasia
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrous dysplasia presents as painless, asymmetric bony expansion in children and adolescents. Its onset tends to occur with periods of high skeletal growth and will often stabilize as skeletal maturity is obtained. [33] Differing functional consequences will occur depending on the bones affected. Malocclusion is often seen with the displacement of teeth, splaying of roots, and crowding. The maxillary sinus may ossify, but more concern focuses on the commonly co-affected zygoma, sphenoid bone, frontal bone, and orbit. Significant fibrous dysplastic changes in this region can lead to blindness. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Retrognathia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In growth and development, there are standard charts commonly used by health care professionals detailing the spectrum of height, weight, age, and growth. It is worth considering there can be significant variation between individuals and that there are both chronologic and biologic ages (considering menarche/puberty). In general, facial height is the last dimension to finish growth and in females will be complete around ages 17-18 years, and in males in the early twenties. In a retrognathic patient or patients who have hypoplastic mandibles, this growth can be done much earlier. Why does this matter? The timing of this growth and development is crucial when considering skeletal surgery to address retrognathia and is carefully ascertained for each patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Craniofacial Growth, And Development -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A cleft lip can be associated with a cleft palate. This is due to an abnormality in the development of the hard palate or roof of the mouth. Bilateral outgrowths from the maxillary prominences are called palatine shelves. These grow inferiorly from the primary palate until the end of the sixth week, when they elevate and fuse along the midline to form the complete secondary palate (hard palate). Failure in this process gives rise to a cleft palate. This is commonly diagnosed in newborn screening or following feeding difficulties and poor weight gain in early infancy. However, a cleft palate can sometimes be diagnosed later in life, for example, through nasal speech.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Craniofacial Growth, And Development -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The most common congenital craniofacial malformation is facial clefting, accounting for 13% of all congenital anomalies. [4]  Failure of any of the facial prominences to fuse can result in facial clefting. These include a cleft lip (failure of the maxillary process and the medial nasal prominence to fuse) [5]  and an oblique facial cleft (failure of maxillary and lateral nasal prominences to fuse).

retrieved_docs.title: Human Growth and Development -- Issues of Concern -- Measurement of Growth
retrieved_docs.content: Dental assessment: Primary tooth eruption begins with the central incisors at 6 months. No single tooth by 13 months of age is of concern. Permanent tooth eruption starts at 6 years of age and continues up to 18 years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Microdontia is a rare dental anomaly characterized by teeth that appear smaller than expected. True generalized microdontia, a condition affecting all teeth, often occurs in individuals with systemic syndromes, such as pituitary dwarfism and orofaciodigital or oculomandibulofacial syndrome. [1] [2] [3] Younger patients who have undergone chemoradiotherapy may also develop microdontia due to disruptions in dental development. [4] True generalized microdontia rarely occurs in individuals without underlying genetic or developmental conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Adenoids -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Adenoid faces: Adenoid faces describes the differences in physical characteristics seen in children with adenoid hypertrophy. The belief is that adrenal hypertrophy results in obstruction in proper nasal breathing, leading to mouth breathing and issues with dental and maxillofacial development. Adenoid faces characteristically demonstrate narrowed maxillary and dental arches, upper lip incompetence, retropositioning of mandibular incisors and hyoid bone,  and a posterior-rotated mandible. There is also a downward tongue displacement and a lower position of the mandible. Removal of excessive adenoid tissue allows for the recovery of proper craniofacial growth. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There are several methods to treat malocclusion. Palatal expanders are traditionally used before braces treatment to treat a narrow palate, crowding, or crossbite. Rapid palatal expansion (RPE) works by placing a force on the two maxillary bones to split the mid-palatal suture. Palatal expanders are most useful during adolescence, as the maxillary bones become fused during puberty. The expander contains a screw turned daily or as directed by the patient's orthodontist. Every turn places pressure on the maxillary bone, allowing for less than 1 mm of expansion. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Implants -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Asymmetries are present in numerous congenital deformities and vary in presentation. Human skull growth occurs in multiple vectors: transverse, sagittal, and vertical. The maxilla typically grows anteriorly, inferiorly, and laterally, while the condyles grow superoposteriorly, pushing the mandible anteroinferiorly. The chin stops growing before the rest of the mandible, which continues to develop transversely and vertically. [11] Deviations from the typical growth pattern introduce mandibular rotation, creating a dolichocephalic (long, retrognathic, and vertical) or brachycephalic (short, prognathic, and horizontal) appearance. Growth usually stops at 14 to 16 years old in female individuals and 16 to 18 in male individuals. [12] Individuals with hypoplastic mandibles can correct the dental malocclusion with orthodontics if present. However, the remaining skeletal defect permits further correction with orthognathic surgery, implant augmentation, or both. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemifacial Microsomia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: MDO effectively lengthens the mandible and improves facial symmetry, appearance, and dental occlusion, as demonstrated by postoperative cephalograms and radiographs. [63] However, long-term outcome data remain limited. Follow-up studies reveal a high recurrence rate. Hollier et al reported recurrence rates between 51% and 100% occurring 42 to 92 months after surgery. Similar recurrence rates requiring revision surgery have been documented in other studies. [64] [65] These findings underscore the importance of ongoing monitoring until skeletal and dental maturity is achieved. In 2012, Mezzini et al found that genetic factors influence the asymmetrical facial growth patterns in HFM patients, which tend to revert to their original asymmetry even after distraction osteogenesis. [66] Counseling patients and families about the significant likelihood of revision surgeries throughout childhood and adolescence remains essential. [67]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertelorism -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgeons usually operate when children are between 5 and 7 years of age. Operating before age 5 can interfere with the development of the maxillary arch and dental structures and present challenges due to underdeveloped bone strength. While surgical outcomes are more predictable in adults, early intervention is often preferred to address the psychosocial impacts of visible facial differences. Coexisting craniosynostosis should undergo correction within the first year of life. Orbital osteotomies are typically paired with soft tissue adjustments to create a more natural facial appearance.

retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2192
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face is a specific type of neoplasm of bone of the face, representing a malignant tumor that originates in the facial bones. This relationship indicates that the broader category “neoplasm of bone of the face” encompasses the more specific entity “primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face,” clarifying the hierarchical classification of facial bone tumors for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is further linked to distinct skeletal malignancies, including malignant neoplasm of the frontal bone, occipital bone, parietal bone, temporal bone and the Arabic‑language malignant tumor of the upper jaw (ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي). Collectively, these relationships highlight the interconnected impact of tobacco exposure, antidepressant therapy, and the diverse malignant entities affecting the skull, facial bones, specific cranial bones and associated lymphatic structures, underscoring the need for comprehensive evaluation and coordinated management in patients with cranio‑facial malignancies.

retrieved_docs.content: Skull and face fractures, described in Arabic as كسر في قبو القحف, refer to injuries involving breaks in the cranial vault and the facial bones, encompassing a range of traumatic damage to the head and facial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: Epistaxis (nosebleed) is classified as a specific type of nasal disorder that falls under the broader category of “nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere,” indicating that it is considered a child condition within this unspecified nasal disorder classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 188
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasms that involve the ethmoid bone, frontal bone, occipital bone, parietal bone, temporal bone, and vomer are each classified as malignant neoplasms of the respective bone, indicating that metastatic spread to these cranial structures is recorded as a primary bone malignancy of that site. A metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the neck that originates from a primary malignant neoplasm of the thyroid is described as a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation, with morphology corresponding to a primary malignant tumor (ورم خبيث أولي). Primary malignant neoplasms of the bone of the face, nasal bone, head of the pancreas, and thymus are likewise characterized as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations and share the same primary malignant tumor morphology. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of nasal bone is described as having the morphology of ورم خبيث أولي (primary malignant tumor). In addition, سرطانة توتية is classified as a type of ورم خبيث أولي, indicating that it too represents a primary malignant tumor. Both the nasal bone neoplasm and سرطانة توتية therefore share the underlying categorization of primary malignant tumor, reflecting their common oncologic identity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are each examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which constitute pathological processes. These entities represent malignant tumor growth arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head, the nasal bone, and the thymus—characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of cancerous cells. As pathological processes, they require oncologic assessment, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies to manage the aggressive nature of the malignancy in each location.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone, metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone (diagnosis) are each classified as malignant neoplasms of the ethmoid bone, frontal bone, and temporal bone respectively. These site‑specific malignant bone tumors are encompassed within the broader diagnostic category “cancer of bone and connective tissue,” which is mapped to each of the three bone‑specific malignant neoplasms. Consequently, the three metastatic bone cancers are recognized as particular manifestations of bone and connective tissue cancer, highlighting their shared pathology while preserving their distinct anatomical locations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.
